Admission Summary: The patient is a 33-year-old female with a mild history of asthma who was admitted to the orthopedic surgical service after presenting to the emergency department following an accidental fall. The patient stated that she was running down a flight of concrete stairs when she missed the final step, causing her right foot to catch and forcibly invert under her full body weight. She experienced an immediate, sharp 'pop' in her right ankle, followed by excruciating pain and an immediate inability to bear any weight on the limb. On physical examination, her right lower extremity was notable for a gross, visible lateral displacement deformity of the foot relative to the tibia. Massive, rapid edema and tracking ecchymosis were present over both the medial and lateral aspects of the ankle joint. The skin was entirely intact, with no tenting or open wounds. Distal neurovascular examination demonstrated strong, bounding dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses, capillary refill time under two seconds, and intact sensation to light touch across all aspects of the foot and toes. An emergent closed reduction was performed in the emergency bay by the orthopedic resident to restore anatomical alignment and protect the soft tissues, and the limb was placed into a well-padded plaster sugar-tong splint. Post-reduction radiographs confirmed persistent widening of the clear space and displacement of the malleoli, requiring inpatient admission for definitive surgical stabilization.
Discharge Summary: Patient Gwen Stacy-Parker was safely discharged home in stable condition on 2026-06-29 following a 5-day inpatient stay for a bimalleolar right ankle fracture. On 2026-06-25, she was taken to the operating room where she underwent an uncomplicated open reduction and internal fixation of the right ankle. Under general anesthesia, anatomical reduction of the fibula was achieved and secured with a 6-hole third-tubular plate, and the medial malleolus was stabilized using two 4.0mm cancellous screws. Intraoperative fluoroscopy confirmed perfect restoration of the ankle mortise and syndesmotic integrity. The surgical incisions were closed using non-absorbable nylon sutures and dressed appropriately, and the patient was placed back into a rigid posterior plaster splint. Post-operatively, her pain was managed effectively using a combination of scheduled intravenous Acetaminophen and oral Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen as needed, completely avoiding morphine. Her pulmonary status remained entirely stable throughout the stay, with no asthma exacerbations noted. By day 3 post-op, the swelling had significantly down-trended, and she successfully passed her physical therapy evaluation for crutch training, demonstrating excellent stability and balance. At the time of discharge, her pain was well-controlled, and her incisions were clean, dry, and non-draining. She was discharged with instructions to remain strictly non-weight-bearing on the right leg, keep the splint completely clean and dry, and elevate the leg above the level of the heart as much as possible. She will follow up in the orthopedic clinic in two weeks for suture removal and transition into a short-leg walking boot.

Serial troponins remained completely flat, confirming that his mild chest tightness was a manifestation of stable angina in the setting of extreme afterload rather than an acute plaque rupture. His renal function stabilized with a creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL. He is safe for discharge home.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 65-year-old male with a significant medical history of poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and coronary artery disease post-CABG who presented to the emergency department complaining of a sudden, severe, throbbing occipital headache that woke him from sleep, accompanied by marked dizziness, blurred vision, and a mild, familiar crushing sensation in his chest. The patient admitted to being highly non-compliant with his daily home antihypertensive medication due to a high-stress lifestyle. Upon initial examination in the triage bay, the patient was noted to be extremely agitated and volatile. His initial blood pressure was critically elevated at 210/115 mmHg in both arms, satisfying the criteria for a hypertensive crisis. His heart rate was 92 breaths per minute, respiratory rate was 18 breaths per minute, and he was afebrile. Neurological examination was entirely intact, with no focal motor or sensory deficits, and a negative Babinski sign, ruling out an acute hemorrhagic or ischemic cerebrovascular accident. Fundoscopic exam revealed mild arteriolar nicking but no acute papilledema or retinal hemorrhages, classifying the presentation as hypertensive urgency rather than emergency. Cardio-thoracic examination revealed a regular rhythm, a loud S4 gallop reflecting decreased left ventricular compliance from long-standing pressure overload, and well-healed midline sternotomy scars. Laboratory workup showed stable cardiac enzymes but mild acute kidney injury. The patient was immediately started on oral antihypertensive agents and admitted to the telemetry service for careful, step-wise blood pressure reduction to prevent cerebral hypoperfusion.
Discharge Summary: Patient Jonas Jameson was safely discharged on 2026-06-30 following a 5-day inpatient admission for hypertensive urgency and optimization of his cardiovascular and metabolic regimens. Upon admission, rather than utilizing aggressive intravenous titratable infusions which could cause a dangerous precipitous drop in blood pressure, he was managed with scheduled oral medications. Amlodipine 10mg daily was initiated, and his home beta-blocker was switched to Metoprolol Succinate 50mg daily, ensuring strict avoidance of Carvedilol due to his history of severe asthmatic bronchospasm with that specific agent. Over the course of 96 hours, his blood pressure down-trended smoothly from a peak of 210/115 mmHg to an average discharge reading of 134/78 mmHg. His presenting symptoms of severe headache and blurred vision resolved completely as his blood pressure normalized. Serial Troponin I tests were checked every 8 hours for three consecutive cycles and remained entirely negative, ruling out an acute myocardial infarction. His acute kidney injury resolved with intravenous hydration, with his serum creatinine returning to his baseline of 1.1 mg/dL. His blood glucose levels were stabilized using a modified sliding scale insulin regimen alongside his home Metformin. Prior to discharge, the patient was extensively counseled by the medical team regarding the life-threatening risks of medication non-compliance and the urgent necessity of smoking cessation. He was discharged in a calm, stable state with a restructured oral medication regimen and is scheduled for an outpatient follow-up appointment with Dr. Vance in one week.
Prescription: Amlodipine 10mg PO QD; Metoprolol Succinate 50mg PO QD (Replaced Carvedilol); Lisinopril 40mg PO QD; Metformin 1000mg PO BID; Aspirin 81mg PO QD; Atorvastatin 40mg PO QD; Nitroglycerin 0.4mg SL tabs PRN chest pain.

We will initiate Aspirin this morning for secondary stroke prevention, avoiding all other NSAIDs due to his severe gastric sensitivity.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 42-year-old male with a significant history of congenital blindness and chronic hypertension who was brought to the emergency department via ambulance after experiencing the sudden onset of left-sided body numbness and coordination failure. The patient's legal partner reported that they were working at their law office when the patient suddenly complained of an intense tingling sensation engulfing the entire left side of his face, left arm, and left leg, followed immediately by an inability to stand straight and severe nausea. Upon arrival in the emergency department within two hours of symptom onset, the patient's vital signs were significant for an elevated blood pressure of 178/96 mmHg, a heart rate of 82 beats per minute, and a normal respiratory rate. Neurological examination was notable for baseline complete blindness with non-reactive pupils, but revealed a new, dense loss of sensation to light touch, pinprick, and temperature across the entire left side of his body, alongside a pronounced left-sided pronator drift and significant dysmetria on left-sided finger-to-nose testing. A code stroke was activated immediately. A non-contrast head CT ruled out hemorrhage, and a subsequent CT angiogram identified an occlusion within the P2 segment of the right posterior cerebral artery. Given the clear timeline and absence of contraindications, the decision was made to proceed with intravenous thrombolytic therapy.
Prescription: Aspirin 81mg PO daily (Initiated post-24h CT clearance); Atorvastatin 80mg PO daily; Amlodipine 10mg PO daily (Dose increased); Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg PO daily; Famotidine 20mg PO BID (Gastric protection).

Admission Summary: The patient is a 47-year-old male prominent district attorney with a medical history of hypertension and bipolar disorder who was rushed to the emergency department as a level 1 trauma alert following a severe assault involving the intentional throwing of a highly corrosive acid chemical agent onto his body. Upon presentation, the patient was in extreme agony, tachypneic at 32 breaths per minute, tachycardic at 128 beats per minute, and hypertensive at 174/104 mmHg. Physical examination revealed catastrophic, deep full-thickness chemical burns encompassing the entire left lateral half of his face, left ear, left neck, and the anterior aspect of his left shoulder and upper arm, estimating approximately 20% total body surface area. The burned skin on the left side appeared charred, leathery, and completely anesthetic to pinprick, while the surrounding edges were exquisitely painful and erythematous. Most critically, the patient exhibited significant inspiratory stridor, a hoarse muffled voice, a continuous brassy cough producing thick carbonaceous sputum, and visible soot deposition within his oral cavity and nares, indicating an acute, life-threatening inhalation injury. Due to the high risk of immediate airway loss from rapidly expanding supraglottic edema, a code airway was called, and the patient was successfully intubated via rapid sequence induction. Large-bore intravenous access was secured, aggressive fluid resuscitation based on the Parkland formula was commenced, and he was transferred directly to the ICU.
Discharge Summary: Patient Harvey Dent-Gordon was stable for official discharge from the acute care hospital service on 2026-06-30 to facilitate immediate transfer to a specialized Burn Reconstruction and Rehabilitation Facility after a 10-day critical care admission. During his ICU stay, he was maintained on mechanical ventilation for 5 days due to bronchoscopically confirmed inhalation injury, receiving scheduled airway clearance, humidified oxygen, and continuous sedation. Fluid resuscitation was executed flawlessly with a total of 6,500 mL of Lactated Ringer's solution administered over the first 24 hours, maintaining excellent renal perfusion and avoiding acute kidney injury. On day 3, the Burn Surgery service performed a major operative intervention consisting of tangential debridement of the full-thickness chemical burns on his left face, neck, and upper arm. Due to a documented allergy to silver sulfadiazine, alternative topical treatments utilizing Mafenide Acetate and petroleum-gauze dressings were applied. The surgical sites remained free of secondary bacterial infection. His blood pressure was controlled with his home Lisinopril, and his Bipolar I disorder was carefully managed in conjunction with Psychiatry, keeping his serum Lithium levels strictly within the therapeutic window. At the time of discharge, he was extubated, breathing comfortably on room air, tolerating a soft diet, and able to ambulate with assistance. He was discharged in stable clinical condition specifically for transfer to the specialized tertiary burn unit for ongoing skin grafting and comprehensive physical rehabilitation.
Prescription: Lithium Carbonate 300mg PO TID; Lisinopril 20mg PO QD; Omeprazole 20mg PO QD; Oxycodone 5mg PO q6h PRN severe pain; Gabapentin 300mg PO TID (For neuropathic burn pain); Mafenide Acetate 8.5% topical cream apply to left neck/arm wounds daily.

On physical examination in the emergency bay, he was acutely ill, febrile to 39.9 degrees Celsius, tachycardic at 112 beats per minute, and normotensive. Splinter hemorrhages were noted beneath his fingernails, and a detailed cardiac auscultation revealed a prominent, grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur loudest at the right upper sternal border, which was entirely new compared to his last outpatient records. Abdominal palpation revealed guarding and focal tenderness over the left upper quadrant, with mild splenomegaly. Laboratory assessments revealed a severe systemic inflammatory response, significant leukocytosis, and an isolated thrombocytopenia. Given the strong clinical triad of a structural valve abnormality, persistent fevers, a new murmur, and signs of peripheral embolic phenomena, a code endocarditis protocol was initiated. Multiple sets of blood cultures were drawn, and a subsequent transesophageal echocardiogram confirmed a large, mobile aortic valve vegetation, establishing a definitive diagnosis of infective endocarditis.
Discharge Summary: Patient Peter Quill-Meredith was safely discharged on 2026-06-29 following a highly critical 7-day inpatient stay for MSSA aortic valve endocarditis complicated by a downstream embolic splenic infarction. Upon admission, broad empiric antibiotics were held briefly until multiple blood cultures were secured, which rapidly grew Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. He was immediately placed on a continuous target regimen of high-dose intravenous Nafcillin 2g IV every 4 hours. Vancomycin was strictly avoided. Over his week-long stay, his septic state reversed completely, his inflammatory markers fell significantly, and he remained entirely afebrile for over 96 hours. Serial blood cultures confirmed successful eradication of the bacteremia. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography showed a stable, non-expanding 8mm vegetation with no signs of abscess formation or worsening valvular regurgitation; Cardiothoracic Surgery evaluated the patient and determined that immediate operative intervention was not warranted, provided he remains stable. For discharge, a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) was placed in his right upper arm. He was transitioned to home health services to receive his remaining 5 weeks of continuous IV Nafcillin therapy. He was thoroughly educated on PICC line care, temperature logs, and the absolute requirement for lifetime antibiotic prophylaxis prior to any dental procedures.
Prescription: Nafcillin 2g IV q4h via PICC line (To be administered at home for 35 remaining days); Acetaminophen 500mg PO q6h PRN discomfort; Albuterol HFA 2 puffs q4h PRN wheezing.

His healing rate appears remarkably efficient, with minimal localized edema noted on re-evaluation. His CIWA scores remained below 4 throughout his stay, requiring no benzodiazepines for withdrawal. Discharged stable against further medical observation, with instructions for suture removal in 7 days.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 53-year-old male with a history of chronic alcohol consumption who presented to the emergency department via police escort following a severe physical altercation at a local tavern. Upon arrival, the patient was alert but highly uncooperative and irritable, smelling strongly of alcohol and complaining of a severe throbbing headache. His vital signs were stable, with a blood pressure of 142/86 mmHg, heart rate of 88 bpm, and a normal respiratory rate. Physical examination revealed multiple deep, linear, clean-cut soft tissue lacerations across his anterior chest wall and the dorsal aspects of both forearms, with active capillary bleeding. Pronounced soft tissue ecchymosis and localized swelling were noted over his right zygomatic arch and occipital scalp. He displayed mild confusion and a brief period of post-traumatic amnesia regarding the exact details of the event, satisfying the criteria for a mild traumatic brain injury. Neurological evaluation was otherwise non-focal, with symmetric pupillary responses and intact gross motor function. Laboratory work confirmed an elevated blood alcohol level, while all other parameters were within normal functional limits. Given the extensive nature of the lacerations and the need for head injury observation alongside alcohol withdrawal tracking, he was placed in the ED observation unit.
Discharge Summary: Patient Logan Howlett-Xavier was safely discharged home from the Emergency Observation Unit on 2026-06-27 in stable clinical condition. During his short stay, his multiple cutaneous lacerations were meticulously irrigated with normal saline and repaired under local anesthesia using 3-0 and 4-0 nylon sutures. He was monitored closely using the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) protocol; he did not develop any tremors, diaphoresis, or hemodynamic instability, and his sensorium cleared completely as his blood alcohol level normalized. His post-concussive headache improved significantly with scheduled oral acetaminophen. Prior to discharge, his wounds were inspected and found to be clean, dry, and well-approximated without any signs of hematoma or early infection. The patient was thoroughly counseled on wound care, the necessity of keeping the sutures dry for 48 hours, and was strongly advised to seek counseling for alcohol cessation. He was instructed to return to the clinic in one week for suture removal.
Prescription: Acetaminophen 500mg PO q6h PRN headache; Cephalexin 500mg PO q6h for 5 days (prophylactic wound coverage); Bacitracin topical ointment apply to lacerations daily.

Allergies: Amlodipine (Causes severe, intractable lower extremity pitting edema that mimics heart failure)
Current Medications: Lisinopril 40mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Pioglitazone 30mg daily.
Lab Tests Performed: NT-proBNP (Elevated at 3,800 pg/mL), Serum Creatinine (Increased to 1.9 mg/dL from baseline 1.2), Blood Glucose (210 mg/dL), HbA1c (9.8%), Troponin I (Negative x2).
Radiology Tests: Chest X-ray: Marked cardiomegaly with a classic 'bat-wing' pattern of bilateral alveolar infiltrates, prominent cephalization of pulmonary veins, and bilateral blunting of the costophrenic angles. Arterial Doppler of Lower Extremities: Severe multi-segmental occlusive disease with monophasic flow in the distal posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries bilaterally.

Doctor Notes: Patient presented in a severe state of hypertensive emergency with flash pulmonary edema. His blood pressure upon arrival was a staggering 224/128 mmHg. Intravenous Nitroglycerin titration was highly effective, safely lowering his blood pressure to 150/90 mmHg over the first 4 hours and allowing for successful discontinuation of BiPAP support onto standard room air. He had an excellent diuretic response to IV Furosemide, losing 3.5 kg of fluid weight overnight. His baseline renal function is somewhat impaired, so we must track his creatinine closely during aggressive diuresis. His chronic diabetic foot ulcers will be evaluated by the wound care team today.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 58-year-old male with a significant history of neglected type 2 diabetes, chronic tobacco use, and advanced peripheral vascular disease who was emergently admitted to the cardiology service following a presentation for an acute hypertensive crisis and severe respiratory distress. The patient reported that he was resting when he suddenly developed an overwhelming sensation of suffocation, preventing him from lying flat and causing him to cough up small amounts of pink, frothy secretions, accompanied by a blinding headache and blurred vision. Upon arrival via EMS, he was in profound respiratory distress, orthopneic, diaphoretic, tachypneic at 28 breaths per minute, and severely hypertensive with a blood pressure of 224/128 mmHg. His oxygen saturation was 82% on room air. Physical exam was remarkable for loud bilateral pulmonary crackles extending to the upper lung fields, a prominent S4 gallop, and severe chronic skin changes in the lower extremities including a dry varus deformity of the right foot and a 2cm ischemic ulcer over his left lateral malleolus. Laboratory evaluations confirmed a markedly elevated NT-proBNP and acute kidney injury. A chest radiograph confirmed severe acute pulmonary congestion. Given the acute target-organ damage manifesting as flash pulmonary edema, he was immediately placed on non-invasive mechanical ventilation and started on titratable intravenous vasodilators.
Prescription: Nitroglycerin IV infusion titrated per protocol; Furosemide 80mg IV q12h; Hydralazine 25mg PO q8h (Alternative vasodilator); Metoprolol Succinate 50mg PO daily; Regular Insulin SC sliding scale before meals.

Symptoms: Severe, unremitting palpitations, profound heat intolerance, drenching diaphoresis, fine resting tremors of the hands, extreme emotional lability and agitation, a 10-kilogram weight loss over two months despite hyperphagia.
Past Medical History: Graves' disease (diagnosed 2025, poorly compliant with anti-thyroid therapy), chronic tension headaches, panic disorder.
Allergies: Methimazole (Causes severe, life-threatening agranulocytosis / absolute neutrophil count drops to zero)
Current Medications: Propylthiouracil (PTU) 100mg TID (reported intermittent use), Propropanolol 40mg BID, Alprazolam 0.5mg PRN.
Lab Tests Performed: Free Thyroxine (Free T4) (Critically elevated at >6.0 ng/dL), Free Triiodothyronine (Free T3) (Critically elevated at >20.0 pg/mL), Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone (TSH) (Profoundly suppressed at <0.01 uIU/mL), CBC (WBC 6.2k/uL, normal absolute neutrophil count at 3.5k/uL), BMP (Normal).
Radiology Tests: Thyroid Ultrasound: Demonstrated diffuse, homogeneous enlargement of both thyroid lobes with markedly increased vascularity on color Doppler, consistent with a high-output 'thyroid inferno' pattern of active Graves' thyrotoxicosis.
Treatment Plan: Immediate admission for high-acuity metabolic stabilization; administration of high-dose oral Propylthiouracil (PTU) 200mg every 4 hours (avoiding Methimazole due to past agranulocytosis); initiation of intravenous Propranolol to control life-threatening adrenergic hyperactivity; administration of stress-dose intravenous Hydrocortisone to block peripheral conversion of T4 to T3; strict temperature tracking and avoidance of all iodine exposures until fully blocked.
Doctor Notes: Patient presented with a severe hypermetabolic state scoring 35 on the Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale, indicating impending thyroid storm. She responded beautifully to high-dose PTU, frequent beta-blockade, and hydrocortisone. Her resting heart rate dropped from a dangerous 136 bpm down to a stable 78 bpm, and her severe agitation has completely resolved. Serial Free T4 levels have down-trended to 2.8 ng/dL. Her white blood cell counts were monitored daily and remained entirely stable, showing no signs of marrow suppression. Discharged home stable on a maintenance dose of PTU with a mandatory endocrinology follow-up next week for definitive radioactive iodine or surgical planning.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 36-year-old female with an established history of Graves' disease who was admitted to the general medicine service following an emergency room presentation for an acute hypermetabolic crisis. The patient's spouse reported that over the past week, she had become increasingly agitated, completely unable to sleep, flushed, and complained of a pounding sensation in her chest that felt like it would burst out, alongside severe sweating even in air-conditioned rooms. Upon physical examination, she was in a state of extreme psychomotor agitation, hyper-reflexic, flushed, and actively diaphoretic. Her vital signs revealed a severe sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 136 beats per minute, an elevated blood pressure of 162/94 mmHg, and a low-grade pyrexia of 38.2 degrees Celsius. Marked bilateral exophthalmos (proptosis) and a diffusely enlarged, non-tender thyroid goiter with an audible vascular bruit were easily appreciated on examination. A fine, high-frequency resting tremor was present in both upper extremities. Laboratory evaluations confirmed critical, off-scale elevations in peripheral thyroid hormones with a completely undetectable TSH. Given her high heart rate, fever, and severe neuro-psychiatric agitation, she was managed aggressively to abort a full-blown thyroid storm and admitted for inpatient critical tracking.
Discharge Summary: Patient Jean Grey-Summers was safely discharged home in stable condition on 2026-06-30 following a 7-day inpatient stay for severe Graves' thyrotoxicosis. Upon admission, she was initiated on high-dose oral Propylthiouracil (PTU) 200mg every 4 hours, and her systemic sympathetic overactivity was controlled using scheduled intravenous and oral Propranolol, carefully bypassing Methimazole due to her historic agranulocytosis. Intravenous Hydrocortisone 100mg every 8 hours was co-administered for the first 48 hours to further suppress thyroid hormone release and peripheral conversion. This aggressive regimen successfully aborted the metabolic crisis: her heart rate stabilized into the 70s, her body temperature normalized, and her extreme anxiety and tremors subsided completely. Her thyroid hormone levels were tracked serially and demonstrated a steady downward trajectory. Prior to discharge, she was transitioned to a maintenance oral dose of PTU 100mg three times daily and Propranolol 40mg three times daily. She was thoroughly educated on the warning signs of agranulocytosis (such as a sudden sore throat or fever) and instructed to obtain immediate blood counts if these occur. She is scheduled for a close-interval outpatient endocrinology consultation in one week.
Prescription: Propylthiouracil 100mg PO TID; Propranolol 40mg PO TID; Clonazepam 0.25mg PO BID for residual anxiety; Omeprazole 20mg PO QD.

Past Medical History: Moderate persistent childhood asthma (multiple childhood hospitalizations), seasonal allergies.

Radiology Tests: Chest X-ray: Diffuse hyperinflation, prominent peribronchial cuffing, no focal infiltrates, normal cardiac silhouette size. Electrocardiogram (ECG): Sinus tachycardia at 122 bpm, diffuse non-specific ST-segment and T-wave abnormalities, no localized ST elevations.
Treatment Plan: Intermittent nebulized Albuterol 2.5mg combined with Ipratropium 0.5mg every 3 hours (monitoring heart rate closely and avoiding continuous delivery due to past adverse reactions), intravenous Methylprednisolone 1mg/kg every 12 hours, supplemental humidified oxygen via nasal cannula to maintain SpO2 >94%, continuous telemetry monitoring to track potential arrhythmias from his concurrent acute myocarditis, and a scheduled pediatric echocardiogram.
Doctor Notes: Patient is a 16-year-old adolescent who presented in status asthmaticus but was found to have a concurrent, unexpected elevation in his cardiac troponins, indicating an acute localized myocarditis likely driven by the same viral prodrome that triggered his asthma. Given his dangerous history of severe tachycardia and hypokalemia when exposed to high-dose continuous albuterol, we are managing his bronchospasm with strict, spaced intermittent treatments. His lungs have shown a moderate response today, with wheezing becoming less tight, but his resting heart rate remains elevated at 118 bpm due to the underlying myocardial inflammation. Echocardiogram is pending this afternoon.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 16-year-old adolescent male with an established history of childhood asthma who was brought to the pediatric emergency department by his mother due to a 12-hour history of rapidly progressive shortness of breath, severe chest tightness, and audible wheezing that failed to respond to multiple home rescue treatments. The mother noted that he had a mild upper respiratory infection earlier in the week. Upon presentation, he was tachypneic at 30 breaths per minute, tachycardic at 124 bpm, and maintaining an oxygen saturation of 89% on room air. He was utilizing his accessory neck muscles for respiration and could only speak in short, broken phrases. Auscultation of the chest revealed globally restricted air movement with tight, diffuse, bilateral inspiratory and expiratory wheezing. Initial emergency labs were obtained, which revealed a completely unexpected, significant elevation in cardiac Troponin I at 1.25 ng/mL, raising immediate concern for an inflammatory myocarditis complicating his presentation. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia with diffuse repolarization abnormalities. Given his unique history of severe tachycardia and hypokalemia with continuous albuterol therapy, he was admitted to the pediatric intermediate care service for spaced, closely monitored bronchodilator treatments, systemic steroids, and continuous telemetry.
Prescription: Methylprednisolone 40mg IV q12h; Albuterol-Ipratropium (Duoneb) 2.5-0.5mg nebulized solution q3h scheduled; Supplemental Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula; Cetirizine 10mg PO daily; Pantoprazole 20mg IV daily.

Current Medications: Allopurinol 100mg daily, Melatonin 5mg nightly.

Doctor Notes: The patient possesses an exceptionally robust physical build and a vast history of prior orthopedic injuries. His non-displaced left clavicle fracture is highly stable and does not require surgical fixation. His right knee swelling was secondary to a severe blunt impact contusion with prepatellar bursitis; a bedside aspiration was deferred as there was no sign of infection. He was successfully trained by physical therapy to ambulate safely with axillary crutches while maintaining partial weight-bearing on the right leg. Pain was controlled adequately with Acetaminophen and low-dose Tramadol, with zero gastrointestinal irritation noted. Discharged home stable.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 50-year-old male with an extensive baseline history of complex physical trauma and a severe intolerance to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs who was admitted to the orthopedic service following an emergency room presentation for acute shoulder and knee injuries sustained during a reported accidental fall down a rocky incline. The patient complained of intense localized pain over his left collarbone and an absolute inability to lift his left arm, along with a severe, swelling impact injury to his right knee that prevented normal weight-bearing. Upon examination, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable, though displaying an exceptionally high pain tolerance. Localized tenderness, prominent crepitus, and minor soft tissue tenting were present over the mid-shaft of the left clavicle. The right knee was grossly distended with a large fluctuant effusion, diffuse ecchymosis, and a significantly restricted range of motion secondary to mechanical pain, though the ligamentous complexes (ACL/MCL) appeared stable on stress testing. Distal neurovascular function was entirely intact in all four limbs. Plain radiographs confirmed a non-displaced mid-shaft left clavicle fracture and cleared the right knee of any acute bony avulsions or fractures, requiring a short inpatient stay for multi-modal pain stabilization and mobility adaptation.
Discharge Summary: Patient Bruce Wayne-Gordon was safely discharged home on 2026-06-29 following a stable 4-day inpatient stay for a left clavicle fracture and a severe right knee contusion. Throughout his admission, his left arm was maintained in a figure-of-eight clavicle harness and a shoulder sling to optimize comfort and bone alignment; repeat radiographs prior to discharge confirmed zero secondary displacement of the fracture fragments. His right knee effusion was treated with continuous cold therapy, compression wrapping, and strict leg elevation, resulting in a significant down-trend in swelling and a functional improvement in active range of motion. Due to his history of severe upper gastrointestinal bleeding with NSAIDs, pain control was restricted to a regimen of scheduled oral Acetaminophen and PRN Tramadol, which provided excellent analgesia. He completed multiple physical therapy sessions, demonstrating safe proficiency with crutches and independence in transfers. He was discharged with instructions to maintain strict sling immobilization of the left arm except during gentle elbow extensions, remain non-weight bearing on the right knee, and continue his home Allopurinol. He will follow up in the orthopedic clinic in two weeks for clinical and radiographic re-evaluation.
Prescription: Acetaminophen 650mg PO q6h scheduled for 7 days; Tramadol 50mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough pain (Avoid NSAIDs); Allopurinol 100mg PO daily; Docusate Sodium 100mg PO BID; Melatonin 5mg PO nightly.

Doctor Notes: Patient is an information systems expert with T6 paraplegia who presented with a severe episode of autonomic dysreflexia triggered by a complicated Klebsiella UTI. Her blood pressure upon arrival was a dangerous 185/105 mmHg, which normalized rapidly to 118/74 mmHg within 30 minutes of bladder decompression and topical nitroglycerin paste application. Her infection responded fully to a 5-day course of IV Ceftriaxone. She remained afebrile, and her diaphoresis and headaches resolved completely. Discharged home stable on oral Cefdinir.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 34-year-old female with a history of T6 complete paraplegia and neurogenic bladder who was admitted to the medicine service following an acute emergency room presentation for a hypertensive crisis and signs of autonomic dysreflexia. The patient reported the sudden onset of a blinding, pounding headache, severe facial flushing, drenching sweating across her face and neck, and a cold shivering sensation in her lower body, occurring shortly after she noticed her urine was exceptionally cloudy and difficult to catheterize. On physical examination in the emergency bay, she was profoundly hypertensive with a blood pressure of 185/105 mmHg (her baseline running approximately 100/60 mmHg) and bradycardic at 54 bpm, which is a classic physiological response to a noxious stimulus below the level of a spinal cord injury. Profound sweating and piloerection were noted above the chest wall, while her lower limbs were dry and cool with baseline spastic paraplegia. Palpation of the abdomen revealed a tense, distended urinary bladder, and immediate catheterization yielded 750mL of foul-smelling, turbid urine with an immediate, dramatic drops in her blood pressure. Laboratory workup confirmed a severe lower urinary tract infection, necessitating admission for definitive intravenous antibiotic therapy and monitoring against recurrent dysreflexia events.
Discharge Summary: Patient Barbara Gordon-Vance was safely discharged home in excellent condition on 2026-06-26 following a 5-day hospitalization for a complicated Klebsiella urinary tract infection and associated autonomic dysreflexia. Upon admission, her acute hypertensive dysreflexia was successfully aborted through immediate bladder decompression via an indwelling catheter and temporary local vasodilator therapy. She was treated with a 5-day course of intravenous Ceftriaxone 2g daily. Urine cultures confirmed a high-burden colonization of Klebsiella pneumoniae, which was fully eradicated by the selected cephalosporin. Over the course of her stay, her blood pressures remained entirely stable at her baseline parameters (95-105/60-70 mmHg) without any recurrence of headaches, flushing, or bradycardia. The indwelling Foley catheter was discontinued on day 4, and she successfully transitioned back to her routine schedule of clean intermittent self-catheterization without any retention or discomfort. She was transitioned to oral Cefdinir 300mg twice daily to complete a 10-day total course of antimicrobial therapy. She was thoroughly re-educated on sterile catheterization techniques, aggressive hydration strategies, and the critical importance of immediate bladder emptying at the earliest sign of facial flushing or headache.
Prescription: Cefdinir 300mg PO BID for 5 remaining days; Baclofen 10mg PO TID; Oxybutynin 5mg PO BID; Acetaminophen 500mg PO q6h PRN mild pain; Nitroglycerin 2% topical ointment (Instructed to apply a 1-inch strip to chest only if SBP spikes >160 mmHg prior to emergency services arrival).

Radiology Tests: 12-Lead ECG (Post-Resuscitation): Sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm, massive ST-segment elevations >5mm in leads V2 through V6, confirming an acute anterolateral STEMI. Coronary Angiogram (Emergent): Demonstrated a 100% thrombotic cut-off in the proximal Left Anterior Descending (LAD) coronary artery. Doctor Notes: Patient is a 41-year-old aircraft pilot who survived a sudden cardiac arrest secondary to an acute proximal LAD occlusion. Ventricular fibrillation was successfully terminated in the field with a single 200J biphasic shock by paramedics after 4 minutes of bystander CPR. Post-stenting, he was transferred to the ICU. He has awakened completely, is oriented to person and place, and follows all commands perfectly, indicating excellent cerebral preservation. Hemodynamics are stabilizing on Norepinephrine at 0.04 mcg/kg/min. Ejection fraction is estimated at 40% with anterior wall hypokinesis. Will continue close tracking.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 41-year-old male test pilot with a history of hyperlipidemia who was emergently admitted to the Intensive Care Unit following a sudden cardiac arrest secondary to an acute myocardial infarction. His spouse reported that they were walking when he suddenly clutched his chest, complained of a tearing pressure behind his breastbone, and collapsed to the ground, becoming completely unresponsive and turning cyanotic. Bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation was initiated immediately by a nearby citizen. Upon arrival of emergency medical services six minutes later, the patient was found to be in pulseless ventricular fibrillation. A single 200-Joule electrical shock was delivered, resulting in an immediate return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC). On presentation to the trauma bay, the patient was obtunded but opening his eyes to painful stimuli. His blood pressure was low at 84/48 mmHg, and his heart rate was 106 bpm. A post-arrest 12-lead ECG revealed catastrophic ST-segment elevations across the entire precordial distribution, indicating an acute anterior STEMI. A cardiac alert was activated instantly, and the patient was taken directly to the cardiac catheterization suite where an emergent percutaneous coronary intervention successfully opened a completely occluded left anterior descending artery.
Prescription: Aspirin 81mg PO daily; Ticagrelor 90mg PO BID; Atorvastatin 80mg PO daily; Amiodarone 200mg PO daily; Norepinephrine IV infusion titrated to MAP >65; Pantoprazole 40mg IV daily.

Address: 56 Central City Way, Speed District, Central Province
Assigned Doctor: Dr. Clara Mercer, MD, Emergency Medicine

Symptoms: Severe, diffuse, crushing muscle pain localized to the bilateral quadriceps and calves, profound generalized weakness, passing dark 'tea-colored' urine, mild nausea.
Past Medical History: Excellent physical health, ultra-marathon runner, high-metabolic baseline, no prior surgeries.
Allergies: Statins / Atorvastatin (Triggers immediate, severe, generalized myalgia and dramatic muscle breakdown)
Current Medications: None
Lab Tests Performed: Serum Creatine Kinase (CK) (Critically elevated at 45,000 U/L), Urinalysis (Dark brown color, strongly positive for blood on dipstick but with 0-1 RBCs per high-power field, confirming heavy myoglobinuria), Serum Creatinine (Elevated at 1.4 mg/dL from baseline 0.8), Serum Potassium (4.6 mEq/L), Serum Calcium (8.2 mg/dL).
Radiology Tests: Ultrasound of Bilateral Lower Extremities: Marked intramuscular edema within the rectus femoris and gastrocnemius muscle bellies, completely normal deep venous system with zero thrombi.
Treatment Plan: Aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation utilizing Normal Saline at a high rate of 250-300 mL/hour to maintain an aggressive urine output goal of >200 mL/hour, preventing myoglobin cast precipitation within the renal tubules; serial tracking of serum electrolytes and creatine kinase levels every 6 hours; strict avoidance of all statin medications; bed rest.
Doctor Notes: Patient is an elite runner who presented with a severe case of exertional rhabdomyolysis following an extreme training session. His initial CK was dangerously high at 45,000 U/L, posing an immediate threat to his kidneys. He responded phenomenally well to aggressive volume expansion, producing over 5 liters of urine over 24 hours. His serum CK down-trended rapidly to 8,200 U/L, and his serum creatinine fully corrected back to his baseline of 0.8 mg/dL. His urine has cleared from tea-colored to a normal straw appearance. Safe for discharge with strict instructions to avoid strenuous exercise and maintain massive oral hydration.
Admission Summary: The patient is a 31-year-old male ultra-marathon athlete with no significant past medical history who presented to the emergency department complaining of an acute onset of excruciating, unremitting muscle pain, profound stiffness, and generalized weakness in his bilateral lower limbs that commenced a few hours after an intense, long-distance endurance run. The patient also noted with high anxiety that he had become unable to walk normally and had passed a small volume of dark, tea-colored urine. Upon physical examination, he was afebrile and normotensive, though visibly distressed and tachycardic at 102 bpm. Exquisite, diffuse tenderness to palpation and prominent firmness were noted across the muscle bellies of his bilateral quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, with a slightly restricted active range of motion at the knees due to pain. There were no signs of compartment syndrome, and distal pulses were strong and equal. Discharge Summary: Patient Barry Allen-West was safely discharged home in excellent clinical condition on 2026-06-30 following a 48-hour stay in the Emergency Observation Unit for severe exertional rhabdomyolysis.
